speaker: interviewer
text: Today is November the 15th, 1995. The survivor being interviewed is Henia Bryer. My name is Andrea Brasg. We are in Bloemfontein, South Africa. And the interview is being conducted in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you just spell the surname for me?

speaker: interviewer
text: B-R-Y-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Andrea Brasg. I'm interviewing Henia Bryer. Today's date is November the 15th, 1995. The interview is being conducted in Bloemfontein, South Africa. And interview is conducted in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Henia Bryer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, H-E-N-I-A, B-R-Y-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Hinda Fiszman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's the-- the Jewish name. But they called me Henia, or Hinka, or all sorts of other names-- H-I-N-D-A. I think that appears on-- used to appear on my birth certificate. And Fiszman-- F-I, spelled in Polish S-Z-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: December, 1925.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Radom, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: 69, or nearly 70, but until my birthday, 69. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What are the names of your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother was Miriam. And my father was Elimelech.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their occupations?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was a housewife. She had a big household to supervise. And my father was a successful businessmen. He had a shoe factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any sisters or brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had an older brother, a younger brother, and a younger sister. We were four-- two boys and two girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The older brother was-- his Polish name was Jacek. His Hebrew name was Yehuda Tzvi. My younger brother is Abraham. He's still alive. He's in Switzerland. And my youngest sister was Mila.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your home address?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was Ulica Malczewskiego 11. It's Malczewskiego Street, number 11, in Radom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was a-- an apartment. People didn't live in houses in Europe. They still don't. It was a big apartment, very comfortable. We lived in a nice part of the city. Opposite was the state theater. And it was a nice, comfortable life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about your bedroom?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was white. And my bed was brass-- were these-- it's fashionable now again. And I shared it with my sister, with my little sister, and complained madly about it. Because she was very small. [LAUGHS] And I liked to read at night, late. And I remember my room very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your childhood like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a very happy childhood. I went to school. The first few years of my life I went to a Polish convent school-- very strange. But that's where I landed, because it was a private school. And then afterwards, I went to a Jewish day school. And there I stayed till the war broke out. The school's name was [NON-ENGLISH], which in-- in-- in translated into English means Friends of Wisdom. It was a-- a school where all the subjects were taught in Polish. But we had additional Hebrew studies and the usual, like they do it here-- Hebrew, the Chumash, and so on, and so on, history, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for fun?

speaker: interviewee
text: For fun, we played sports. In summer, we played netball. In winter, there was ice skating and reading. I always used to love to read. And that was-- that's-- and the school work. And that sort of occupied-- and we played musical instruments. Everybody had to play something. We had a piano, and we took lessons. So that also took some time for practicing. And life was good. We were comfortable, well off. We went on holidays every summer when the schools closed for two-- over two months. We went to a resort-- the whole household-- servants and all. And relatives used to come and visit us there. And my father only came for the weekends, because he had to work. And he couldn't take off for-- for 2 1/2 months. He used to come on Friday and go back on Sunday. And-- and that was the life that was before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about grandparents, aunts?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's parents were dead by now. And my mother's parents, fortunately, went to Israel just before 1938. They-- they lived and they stayed there all the time. We still found them later on. My mother's family-- she had four brothers and a sister in Israel. And the rest of the family remained in Poland. My father's family was in Poland. He had two sisters with their extended families. I don't even know how many there were, because one of them lived in a different town. But the one that lived in our town with her husband and two children all perished; my mother's older sister-- also killed in the-- in the camps. And 1/2 of my family perished in the camps later on. But before the war, there was a big family in Poland on the side of my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your religious background like?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a very traditional Jewish home. But we spoke Polish at home. Festivals were observed. The factory was closed on Saturday. And my father used to go to shul, sometimes on Saturday morning, sometimes Friday night, whenever he could, and on all the festivals and holidays, and obviously, high holidays. And we observed all the festivals. I remember especially Pesach was a-- a fantastic-- Passover was a fantastic night-- grandparents and the whole family, plus aunts, and uncles, and cousins. And there were lots of us at the table. And it took-- by the time my grandfather was finished with the prayers and with the reciting, it was-- we were all asleep. But it was beautiful. And all the other festivals were-- were also observed. It was a-- it was-- it was nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have both Jewish and non-Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly Jewish friends. When I was small and-- and was at the Polish school, I had some Polish friends. But they never came to my house, and I didn't go to their house. So mostly, my friends were Jewish later on. And that's how it remains.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your first experience of anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, before the war, you mean? Before the war, I didn't really have any-- I knew there was anti-Semitism. I heard the older people talking about it. And there was. I knew that the-- that Jewish young people were not allowed to attend university in Poland. They had some restrictions about that. And we-- and-- but I personally never encountered any anti-Semitism. I didn't look Jewish. And everybody took me for a non-Jewish girl. So personally, I didn't sort of encounter anything. But I knew it was there. And-- and there was lots of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] What was your first experience-- how did the war begin for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The war started-- we were supposed to be back at school on the 1st of September, 1939. But we were on holiday. And we were coming back from holiday to be back in time for school. Because at that stage, all-- all the children were at school except, my older brother, who was physically handicapped. He couldn't walk without an aid. But otherwise, he was quite normal. And he always had teachers at home who came to teach him. But the three of us were all at school already. And we had to be back in time for school. But there was tremendous panic the few days before the invasion of Poland, and people were rushing home from wherever they were. Because it was holiday time. And I remember the train trip-- lots of people and lots of panic. And I remember being upset, because I lost my dog on that train. That was the only concern I had at that stage. And when we came home, it took about two days when the bombings-- when the bombs started to fall. And for seven days, the town was bombed. And after a week, the Germans came in the beginning of September. And they marched in like in all the other towns. And from that minute on, the life of everyone changed tremendously.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you explain that further?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I can. First of all, the schools were closed. So-- but we didn't stop learning, because there were lots of teachers in town without work. And so we had teachers coming to the house. They took us in small groups. And we carried on with schoolwork for as long as we could at the beginning of the occupation, while the Germans were still sorting themselves out and sort of settling in. So that was that. But straight from the beginning, there were different-- there-- there were-- there was a tremendous change in our lifestyle. First of all, we had to wear the armbands with the-- with the Star of David on them. And there were millions of restrictions and decrees coming out every single day-- a curfew in the evenings. We're not allowed to get out-- to come out of the house, although we were still lucky to be in our own home. Part of it was requisitions by a German officer. And he lived in part of our apartment. The rest we had to share. So it wasn't as comfortable as before. But still, we somehow managed. And everybody had to work straight from the beginning. So my father had a shoe factory. And they needed him for the war effort. So he carried on manufacturing shoes. But obviously, it was for the Germans now, not for-- for customers. And he didn't get paid either. The-- the-- all the leather that he had and all this stuff was just requisitions. And he had to supply the stuff that they wanted. And we-- we were busy studying whatever we could. But outside, it became very ugly. There were roundups. Especially they collected all the religious Jews that were wearing these black coats and-- and yarmulkes and-- and looked to them strange. They were beating them. They were arresting them. They were torturing people. We heard of people being arrested for no reason whatsoever. And then there were these awful decrees every couple of weeks, every couple of days. One day, there was a decree, we must give up all the silver in the house. So-- and-- and our neighbors were very keen on informing on us too in those days. So they knew exactly where the people lived who had such things that they wanted. And they just took it. One day, they arrived at the house, and they took all our new furniture. They just loaded them on trucks and-- and went away. Then they came and took my piano. I had a concert piano in the house-- took that away. Then there was a decree we must give up all the furs. Now, everybody had furs. It was cold there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother said, no ways is she going to give up her beautiful Astrakhan or Kaliko Russian coat. She is not going to. And we pleaded with her, because if they found you doing something against the law, you could be killed in a minute. But she was determined. She's not going to-- to give her coat. So we spent the whole night cutting up this magnificent coat and burning it in the stove, piece by piece. And in the morning, we took all the other coats. We gathered them. And we gave them away. That was the story of the fur coat.

speaker: interviewee
text: All terrible things happened. And food rationing-- that was-- the poor people were-- were very in-- in-- in bad condition. There was no food. And if you couldn't sell something on the black market, then really, there was very little to eat. We were lucky, I suppose, in a way, because we had lots of things to sell. My mother's jewelry was sold bit by bit. My father had a collection of gold coins, which were sold bit by bit. It lasted till actually, till 1944, when we were sent to a concentration camp. We lived on these things. Obviously, they paid very little for it. We got a fraction what it was worth. But at least there was some food in the house till the last minute. And as I said, life was-- was different, all together-- restrictions, and different orders, and harassment all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the ghetto actually set up?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Radom, it was comparatively late. It was in 1941. There were two ghettos. Radom-- let me tell you a little bit about the town-- was an industrial city. And the Jewish population, according to statistics, as far as I can remember, was 30,000 to 35,000 Jews in town. But during the first two years, that swelled to about 40,000, because there were a lot of people coming from other ghettos. There were ghettos already established, and people were coming to-- to Radom thinking they could find work there easier. Because if you worked, you had a chance of staying in the ghetto. If you didn't have work, you were just rounded up and sent away to one of the death camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were a lot of-- we called them the foreigners-- from Lublin, from Lodz, from Krakow, from Warsaw-- who came to Radom during the first two years of-- of the war and the-- the places where they-- most of them worked. But that was later on in-- in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were two ghettos. The one was in a-- in a-- in another-- on the other side of town, called Glinice. And there was the second. The big ghetto was in the old town, where we were-- where we were actually transferred there. They had in Radom a big armament industry. There were factories manufacturing arms and ammunition. And before the war, mostly non-Jewish people worked there. It was-- but during the war, when the Germans took over all these factories, they used slave labor. They used the Jews. And they worked very hard. My younger brother actually worked there later on in one of the arms factory. The other industry was tanneries, where they cured hides, and make leather-- and industries that are connected. There are a lot of like shoe shops. And my-- my father had a shoe factory too. So they worked in those, because they were all requisitions. And they used slave labor for the war effort in-- in all these factories. Now, the ghetto was established in 1941. That was a big ghetto. Still, there were two ghettos. But then in 1942, was a big, what the Germans call [NON-ENGLISH], which is operation, and Polish it was called [NON-ENGLISH], which is resettlement. But in fact, it was rounding up of thousands of people and sending them to mostly death camps. There were three main death camps in-- in Poland. And the one was Auschwitz. The one was Majdanek. And the third one was Treblinka. There were others too, but these were the main ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after 1942, where they liquidated the other ghetto, there was only one ghetto. And we were-- we had to move out and go to the ghetto there. We were given one room for the whole family, and we were lucky at that. And-- and then, another family didn't have accommodation. So we took them in as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there's a-- a-- a funny story about that. I didn't remember that at all. But I contacted the girl that was the daughter of that family a little while ago. And she reminded me of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I asked her if she remembered me. And she said, how could I ever forget that we lived with you? You took us in. And I never-- I didn't even remember it. But that was-- it was-- it was hard-- one room for a big family and an additional family. But still, we were alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: And everybody had to work. That was the-- the most important thing. Otherwise, they rounded you up and-- and they sent you away. There were also terrible scenes in the ghetto. They were knocking on your door, just hauling people out, shooting them in the street, or in the courtyard, or-- or anywhere at all. It was-- it was horrible. Now at that stage, I still didn't have work. I didn't work till that stage. Until one day, they came. And they said that all the sick people must go to the hospital. They're going to look after them-- not the sick, the handicapped-- sorry. And my brother was taken. They had to take him, because otherwise, the police would come and take them by force. And my older brother, I mentioned he was physically handicapped. He was taken away. And my mother went with him. And it was cold. It was winter. And he-- he took his coat off and gave it to my mother, and said, where I'm going, I won't need it. Give it to somebody who will need it. And it was the last thing we heard of him. They shot all of them outside the town. And that was my older brother. Then we came home. And I had to look for a job. And my younger brother, they managed to get him a place in the-- in the armament factory. And he was still very young. He was nearly two years younger than I am. And he-- [SIGHS] I don't know how he managed there, but he-- apparently, people were saying that he was standing and sleeping by these machines that they had to feed with-- with steel, and do all this. I've never been to this factory. I only heard what they did. But somehow, he survived too. But that's a different story. Now, I had to look for-- for something to do. My father was working. My mother was working in a-- in a-- in a workshop where they were sewing something. But I wasn't-- I wasn't working. So we were looking for-- for work for me. And we heard that there was army supply stores, which was outside the town. And they were looking for people who could sew-- machine-- machine workers. Now I haven't-- I didn't have a clue how to even thread a machine. But I applied for the job, because it was-- one was desperate.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I said, well, what am I going to do? They're going to put me at the machine. And I haven't got a clue how to even thread a machine. What am I going to do? So my mother was a very, very energetic person, and very-- she kept the family together all the time. She says, don't worry. We'll teach you tonight. So she got another woman there. They brought a sewing machine. And they showed me how to use a machine. And that was all-- one lesson.

speaker: interviewee
text: The following morning, I went to this place. I applied for the job. And I got it. And we had to sleep in this place. We didn't come back home. I was there for-- for quite a few months, nearly the whole of-- '40-- '40-- sort of end of '42 and beginning of '43, I was at that place. It was called [NON-ENGLISH]-- which was [NON-ENGLISH]. It was an army supply store. They had different kinds of work. They had men and women working there. But the place where I worked was a-- a-- a workshop where we all did sewing on the machine. We actually had to repair uniforms of soldiers that were coming or going to the front, to the eastern front. And we had to-- to sew these things. And there was a very cruel man in charge of this workshop. I remember his name. I see him in front of me. His name was Weitzman. And he was very, very cruel, and he demanded perfect work. And if something was just not up to standard, he used to beat the people terribly. And if he took a dislike to somebody-- there was a little girl, a friend of mine, who he just disliked. And he used to kick her and beat her for-- for-- for no reason at all. And each time a needle broke-- you know, these things were hard, and-- and-- and sometimes very, very difficult to-- to sew-- then you got punished for that as well. I don't know how, but I managed. I learned quickly. And the other thing was this was a-- a-- an electric powered machine. And I was taught on a-- on a little hand thing. But I think fright and everything else, I managed to do my job. And I stayed there for-- till I-- till 1943, the beginning of-- 1943-- no-- the middle perhaps. I-- I can't remember exactly. But I did come back to the ghetto eventually, when that was-- that place was liquidated. And all the people-- they kept the Polish people. But the Jewish people, they dismissed. And they were all sent to different-- different camps. And I was sent back to the ghetto. And at that stage, the ghetto was very small. Most of the people were sent away already by 1943, and-- to different concentration camps, to death camps, and to everywhere else. There was just a very small workshop in which both my parents worked. And my little sister was still with them. My younger brother was sent somewhere away, somewhere else. My older brother was already dead. So there were just the four of us left. And they both worked. My little sister didn't work. And I was sent back to work in that workshop where my parents were.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was-- I was given a job in a-- they had-- they had a shirt factory there that they made shirts for the Germans. They had a shoe works, and my father was busy there; and some other workshops, I can't remember exactly what it was-- some leather things. And-- but I-- I remember what I was doing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in charge of that workshop were two men, not from Radom, funnily enough. They were experts. The one had a shirt factory in Lodz. And I remember even their names. They-- they were nice, very lovely people. They were so patient with us, the youngsters that were looking there, and showing us everything, and-- and helping us. Because we really didn't have a clue. And this was a-- a new thing all together, to-- to make shirts. But we didn't sew them. We folded them. And we were-- we were-- we were helping in that, just so that we were busy. And the one man was Szwarcbart. And the other one was Cukerman. They were both not from Radom. They were lovely, lovely people. And one-- one evening before we went to our lodgings there-- it was one room for two families at that stage-- one of the girls forgot to switch off the iron. And when we came the next morning, the damage wasn't all that great. But there was damage. The-- the board was burnt. And the iron was burnt out. And the-- the Germans, the SS, came in. And they took these two lovely men. They took them down into the courtyard. And they shot them in front of all of us. It was horrible. This was the first time I've seen something like that happen. Really, it was terrible. And it was an everyday occurrence. That-- that was it.
